m_Item1.QuestionText:
// File: src/com/example/Test.java
package com.example;

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello");
    }
}
Command: javac -d . src/com/example/Test.java What is the result?
What is the result?

Options.OptionText: Compilation error due to package mismatch
Class file placed in current directory

m_Item1.Explanation: The -d . option tells javac to place the compiled class in a directory matching the package structure. So com/example/Test.class is created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command sets the classpath when running a Java program?
m_Item1.Explanation: Both -cp and -classpath are valid options to set the classpath when running a Java program. So, option 4 is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why would you use static import in Java?

Options.OptionText: To import instance variables from another class
To use static members without class reference

m_Item1.Explanation: Static import in Java is used to import static members (fields and methods) so that they can be used without referring to their class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of using packages in Java?

Options.OptionText: To group related classes and interfaces together.
To enable Java programs to interact with databases.
To define the scope of variables within a class.

m_Item1.Explanation: Packages in Java are used to organize classes and interfaces into namespaces, making it easier to manage large software projects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following package is automatically imported in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The java.lang package is automatically imported by the Java compiler and is considered the default package for basic language classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How do you declare a package in a Java source file?
m_Item1.Explanation: The correct syntax for declaring a package in Java is by using the 'package' keyword followed by the package name, ending with a semicolon.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why should you avoid using default (unnamed) packages in your Java projects?

Options.OptionText: They cannot be used in interfaces.
They make it difficult to manage large projects.
They are deprecated in Java 9 and later.

m_Item1.Explanation: Default packages do not provide any group naming conventions, making it harder to manage and organize complex projects. It is better to use named packages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to access classes and interfaces from another package?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'import' keyword is used to import classes and interfaces from another package so they can be accessed in your code.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if two imported packages contain a class with the same name?

Options.OptionText: Java will automatically merge the classes.
Java will generate a compile-time error.
The developer must use the fully qualified class name.

m_Item1.Explanation: To resolve the ambiguity, you must use the fully qualified name (package + class name) to avoid confusion between classes with the same name from different packages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about using subpackages in Java?

Options.OptionText: Subpackages do not exist in Java.
Importing a package automatically includes its subpackages.
Subpackages automatically inherit classes from their parent package.

m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, importing a package does not import its subpackages. Each subpackage must be imported explicitly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a potential downside of using wildcard imports (e.g., import java.util.*)?

Options.OptionText: It makes the code difficult to refactor.
It can lead to naming conflicts.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using wildcard imports can lead to naming conflicts if multiple packages contain classes with the same name, making it harder to identify which class is being used.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a valid package name in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Package names in Java cannot start with a number; they must begin with a letter or underscore and conform to a valid identifier.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following package hierarchies is correctly ordered in Java naming conventions?
m_Item1.Explanation: According to Java naming conventions, packages are named starting with the organization (usually in reverse domain name notation), followed by the project, and then specific modules.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the significance of having a 'package-info.java' file in a package?

Options.OptionText: It provides automatic imports for the package.
It serves as the package's main file.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'package-info.java' file is used to provide documentation and package-level annotations for a package.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the Java compiler locate a package used in a Java program?

Options.OptionText: It uses the current directory only.
It searches only within the JDK installation.
It uses the CLASSPATH environment variable.
It searches the internet for the package.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Java compiler uses the CLASSPATH environment variable to locate where the packages are stored on the filesystem.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What should be the directory structure of a package 'com.company.project'?
m_Item1.Explanation: The directory structure should match the package hierarchy, so 'com.company.project' should be stored in 'com/company/project'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why is it advantageous to use package-private classes?

Options.OptionText: It limits access to the same package.
It increases the visibility to all packages.

m_Item1.Explanation: Package-private classes (classes without an access modifier) are accessible only within the same package, helping to encapsulate classes within a module.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement about the 'java.awt' package is correct?

Options.OptionText: It provides utilities for file handling.
It offers classes for GUI components.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'java.awt' package is part of the Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT) that contains classes for creating graphical user interfaces (GUI) in Java applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these access specifiers must be used for main() method?
m_Item1.Explanation: main() method must be specified public as it called by Java run time system, outside of the program. If no access specifier is used then by default member is public within its own package & cannot be accessed by Java run time system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is used as default for a member of a class if no access specifier is used for it?
m_Item1.Explanation: Default access modifier for a member of class if no modifier is mentioned, is package. It is accessible within the same package

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements are incorrect?

Options.OptionText: public members of class can be accessed by any code in the program
private members of class can only be accessed by other members of the class
private members of class can be inherited by a sub class, and become protected members in sub class
protected members of a class can be inherited by a sub class, and become private members of the sub class

m_Item1.Explanation: private members of a class can not be inherited by a sub class

m_Item1.QuestionText: All the variables of class should be ideally declared as ?
m_Item1.Explanation: The variables should be private and should be accessed with get and set methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following modifier means a particular variable cannot be accessed within the package?
m_Item1.Explanation: Private variables are accessible only within the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can a protected modifier be accessed?

Options.OptionText: Accessible within package and outside the package but through inheritance only

m_Item1.Explanation: The protected access modifier is accessible within package and outside the package but only through inheritance. The protected access modifier can be used with data member, method and constructor. It cannot be applied on the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: import static is used to import?

Options.OptionText: only static and final members in user defined class
only static methods in built in class
import static members in java class

m_Item1.Explanation:
In Java, static import concept is introduced in 1.5 version. With the help of static import, we can access the static members of a class directly without class name or any object.
 For Example: we always use sqrt() method of Math class by using Math class i.e. Math.sqrt(), but by using static import we can access sqrt() method directly

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following directory structure:
bigProject
|--source
| |--Utils.java
|
|--classes
|--
And the following command line invocation:
javac -d classes source/Utils.java

Assume the current directory is bigProject, what is the result?

Options.OptionText: If the compilation is successful,Utils.classis addedtothesourcedirectory.
The compiler returns an invalid flag error
If the compilation is successful,Utils.class is added to the classes directory.
If the compilation is successful,Utils.class is added to the bigProject directory.

m_Item1.Explanation: -d flag is used to save generated class file in the path given if no explicit package is required. So if Utils.java gets compiled successfully, it is kept in classes directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
package test;
 class Target {
      public String name = "hello";
 }
What can directly access and change the value of the variable name?

Options.OptionText: any class in the test package

m_Item1.Explanation: As class is not declared with any access specifier, it is considered as package scope. So all the classes from package test can access name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: You want subclasses in any package to have access to members of a superclass. Which is the most restrictive access that accomplishes this objective?
System class is defined in .................
m_Item1.Explanation:
The java.lang.System class contains several useful class fields and methods. It cannot be instantiated.Facilities provided by System −
  1. Standard output
  2. Error output streams
  3. Standard input and access to externally defined properties and environment variables.
  4. A utility method for quickly copying a portion of an array.
  5. A means of loading files and libraries

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the correct syntax for suggesting that the JVM performs garbage collection
m_Item1.Explanation:
The java.lang.System.gc() method runs the garbage collector. Calling this suggests that the Java Virtual Machine expend effort toward recycling unused objects in order to make the memory they currently occupy available for quick reuse.

Syntax of java.lang.System.gc() method :: public static void gc()
Example:
import java.lang.*;
public class SystemDemo {

   public static void main(String[] args) {

      int arr1[] = { 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };
      int arr2[] = { 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 };
    
      // copies an array from the specified source array
      System.arraycopy(arr1, 0, arr2, 0, 1);
      System.out.print("array2 = ");
      System.out.print(arr2[0] + " ");
      System.out.println(arr2[1] + " ");
      
      // it runs the GarbageCollector
      System.gc();
      System.out.println("Cleanup completed...");
   }
}
Let us compile and run the above program, this will produce the following result −
array2 = 0 10
Cleanup completed...

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is not true in java language ?

Options.OptionText: A public member of a class can be accessed in all the packages.
A private member of a class cannot be accessed by the methods of the same class.
A private member of a class cannot be accessed from its derived class.
A protected member of a class can be accessed from its derived class.

m_Item1.Explanation: A private member can be accessed within the same class by it's methods. So option B is not true.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is a mechanism for naming and visibility control of a class and its content?
m_Item1.Explanation: Packages are both naming and visibility control mechanism. We can define a class inside a package which is not accessible by code outside the package.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of following access specifies can be used for members of a class so that it's access is restricted by the classes in the same package?
m_Item1.Explanation: No modifier means package scope - it is used for restricting access in package

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is incorrect statement about packages?

Options.OptionText: Package defines a namespace in which classes are stored
A package can contain other package within it
Java uses file system directories to store packages
A package can be renamed without renaming the directory in which the classes are stored

m_Item1.Explanation: A package can be renamed only after renaming the directory in which the classes are stored.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following is/are true about packages in Java?
1) Every class is part of some package.
2) All classes in a file are part of the same package.
3) If no package is specified, the classes in the file go into a special unnamed package
4) If no package is specified, a new package is created with folder name of class and the class is put in this package.
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, a package can be considered as equivalent to C++ language’s namespace..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is correct way of importing an entire package ‘pkg’?
m_Item1.Explanation: * indicates all classes from the package.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an incorrect statement about packages?

Options.OptionText: Two different packages can have the class with the same name
A package can contain other package within it
Java uses file system directories to store packages
A package can be renamed without renaming the directory in which the classes are stored

m_Item1.Explanation: A package can be renamed only after renaming the directory in which the classes are stored

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given that  the  root directory contains  a subdirectorv  called "office"  that contains  some files for a Java application, if  "X" and "Y" are unknown arguments, and the following command  is invoked  from  the  root directory in order to create  a jar  file containing  the office  directory: jar  -cf X  Y,     Which are true?

Options.OptionText: X should be the file name of the jar file, and Y should be "office".
X should be "office", and Y should be the file name of the jar file
Specifying a file name of the jar file here is optional.
If a file name is not specified here, a file named office.jar will be created.

m_Item1.Explanation: When cf option is used for jar command, we should specify name of the jar file followed by the contents of the jar file which is a "office" directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statement is true about jar command?

Options.OptionText: The jar command creates the META-INF directory implicitly.
The jar command creates the MANIFEST.MF file implicitly.
The jar command would not place any of your files in META-INF directory.
All of the above are true

m_Item1.Explanation: jar command implicitly creates META-INF folder and creates manifest file if it is not specifically added. User files will not be added in META-INF folder. So option D is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true in terms of the java.lang.Math.floor method?

Options.OptionText: Four overloaded versions of floor exist.
An ArithmeticException is declared in the throws clause.
The type of the return value depends on the argument type.
The returned value is always of type double.

m_Item1.Explanation: Math.floor(double) is the only method available which returns always double value and does not thorw any exception.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The use of protected keyword to a member in a class will restrict its visibility as follows:

Options.OptionText: Visibility only in the class and its subclasses in the same package
Visibility only inside the same package
Visibility in all classes in the same package and subclasses in other packages
Visibility only in the class where it is declared.

m_Item1.Explanation: Protected members are accessible to class itself and all the subclasses in any package.

m_Item1.QuestionText: jar stands for _____________.
m_Item1.Explanation: jar stands for java archive which is a compression format for java classes and support files required for an application.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which  of the following is used to see the details of the compilation.
m_Item1.Explanation: -verbose option gives the details about execution of command
